Title: Customs seizes frozen chicken, pork worth RM2.23 million at Port Klang
Article:
KLANG: The Customs Department has seized four containers of frozen chicken and pork worth RM2.23 million, including duties and taxes, at the North Port Free Zone in Port Klang.
Selangor Customs director Mohamad Azhar Ahmad Paharazi said the seizure was made on Feb 6 under Ops Petasan following intelligence from the National Targeting Centre.
He said enforcement officers inspected four 40-foot refrigerated containers and discovered 112,880kg of frozen meat believed to have been imported without valid permits.
"Three of the containers contained 84,000kg of frozen chicken, estimated to be worth RM1.09 million, with duties and taxes amounting to RM218,400.
"The fourth container held 28,880kg of frozen pork, valued at RM924,160," he said in a statement today.
Azhar said the importation of frozen meat products such as chicken and pork is subject to permits issued by the Malaysian Quarantine and Inspection Services Department (Maqis), as stipulated under the Customs (Prohibition of Imports) Order 2023.
The containers arrived at Port Klang on Jan 16 and Jan 20, with the shipments originating from a country in Asia.
He said the syndicate is believed to have used false declarations in Customs Form 1, labelling the goods as "frozen foodstuff" to evade detection by authorities.
"The case is still under investigation," he added.
Members of the public with information can contact the Customs toll-free hotline at 1-800-88-8855 or the nearest Customs office.
Informants' identities will be kept confidential.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Jaywalking Singaporean hit by e-hailing car in JB
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A Singaporean was injured after he was struck by a car while attempting to cross a busy road here, in an incident that has since gone viral on social media.
Johor Baru (South) police chief Assistant Commissioner Raub Selamat said the accident occurred at 3.40pm on April 3 at Km 2, Jalan Tun Abdul Razak.
Preliminary investigations found that a Malaysian e-hailing driver was travelling along the stretch when the Singaporean suddenly dashed across the road.
"The driver was unable to avoid the pedestrian," he said in a statement.
The Singaporean, working as a tour agent, sustained injuries to his head, arms and legs. He is receiving treatment at the republic's National University Hospital.
The 30-second dashcam footage showed a man, carrying a briefcase, attempting to cross the road despite a pedestrian bridge being just a few metres away.
He reached the midpoint of the two-lane road before suddenly turning back.
The e-hailing driver, who had already switched to the right lane to avoid him, was unable to brake in time when the man ran back into his path.
Raub said investigations are ongoing and urged members of the public to adhere to road regulations, particularly when crossing roads.
"Pedestrians are advised to use designated crossing points and avoid crossing recklessly, as it endangers not only themselves but also other road users," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-sales executive pleads not guilty to over RM1mil fraud charges
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A former sales executive of a housing company pleaded not guilty at the Sessions Court on Tuesday to five counts of criminal breach of trust involving more than RM1 million.
Rafidah Abdul Hamid, 48, pleaded not guilty after the charges were read before Sessions Court judge Datuk Che Wan Zaidi Che Wan Ibrahim.
The accused is alleged to have misappropriated company funds ranging from RM73,000 to RM317,610 for her personal use in each of the five charges, totalling over RM1 million.
The mother of three is said to have committed the offences at a building in Jalan Ibrahim between May 30, 2022, and October 15 last year.
The charges, framed under Section 408 of the Penal Code, carry a jail term of one to 14 years, with whipping and a fine, upon conviction.
The case came to light after the company's 63-year-old manager lodged a police report on March 31.
The report followed a call from a law firm regarding a property sale and purchase agreement involving two individuals.
An internal audit revealed no official records of the transaction in the company's system or accounts.
Investigations showed that the accused, who joined the company in 2021, allegedly received payments from a buyer in January 2024 but failed to surrender the funds to the accounts department or deposit them into the company's official account.
She is also accused of forging a company officer's signature on sale and purchase documents prepared by the law firm to facilitate the transaction.
The accused is believed to have used the same modus operandi to collect money from another individual during the same year.
The court granted bail at RM45,000 with one surety.
The court fixed May 6 for mention.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australian PM to discuss fuel supplies in Singapore visit
Article:
SYDNEY: Australia's Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said Tuesday he will fly to Singapore to help secure petroleum imports as oil prices surge during war in the Middle East.
Australia relies on imports for an estimated 90 per cent of its refined petroleum products, and Singapore is its largest single supplier.
Albanese said he would travel to Singapore from April 9-11 to discuss trade in "essential supplies" such as diesel and liquefied natural gas with Prime Minister Lawrence Wong.
"Together, we share concern over the situation in the Middle East, including the consequences for both of our nations," the Australian leader told reporters in Canberra.
"Australia is facing a more uncertain world, and therefore the certainty of those clear, close relationships is more important than ever."
Australia and Singapore committed in a joint statement last month to keep fuel flowing between both countries and to work together to strengthen energy supply chain resilience.
Service station outages of diesel and unleaded gasoline in Australia are falling, the government said.
A total 241 service stations across Australia – three percent of the total – had no diesel on Tuesday, while 141 had no gasoline.
The country has 29 days' worth of diesel, 39 days of petrol, and 30 days of jet fuel, said energy minister Chris Bowen, stressing that levels had remained "pretty static."
Last week, the government halved its fuel tax on petrol for three months to help alleviate rising costs for motorists and truck drivers.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man killed in parang attack in Buntong, several injured
Article:
IPOH: A 25-year-old man was killed and several others injured in a parang attack in Buntong last night, police said.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Muhammad Najib Hamzah said a man has been arrested, while police continue efforts to track down other suspects.
Police were alerted to a fight in Buntong at 11.10pm yesterday, he said, adding that on arrival, personnel found a man lying in the road with severe injuries from a sharp weapon.
The victim had earlier been at a car rental premises with friends when a group armed with parangs attacked them, leaving several injured.
Several of the victim's friends are being treated at Raja Permaisuri Bainun Hospital.
The case is being investigated for murder, grievous hurt, and rioting.
Police have urged anyone with information to contact the nearest police station or Ipoh district headquarters.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mindef: Defence projects, including Navy LCS, to proceed as scheduled
Article:
LUMUT: The Defence Ministry will continue all major projects as scheduled, including the Royal Malaysian Navy's (RMN) Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) projects, despite the ongoing global energy crisis.
Its deputy minister, Adly Zahari said operational readiness and national security would not be compromised.
"At this point, we are not postponing any projects. Any aspect involving national security, sovereignty, and future planning will proceed as scheduled," he said.
He spoke to reporters after launching the Malaysian Armed Forces Autism Initiative Programme at Laluan Kasih, RMN community-based rehabilitation centre, here today.
He said current projects, including the LCS, are being implemented according to the 13th Malaysia Plan timeline.
"We want to ensure these projects are completed as planned, because delays would result in greater financial impact," he added.
On Saturday, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said that a review and reassessment of Malaysia's growth rates and mega projects must be conducted if the conflict in the Middle East continues to escalate.
Adly also said that safeguarding national sovereignty and optimising defence personnel capabilities remain the ministry's top priorities.
"For now, all planned projects will continue," he said, adding that the ministry has identified certain departments and personnel eligible for work-from-home arrangements and energy-saving measures, in line with directives from the prime minister.
"We support these cost-saving efforts while exploring additional measures to manage expenditures prudently.
"However, operational activities will not be affected. Whether on land, at sea, or in the air, operations are critical to national security, and we will continue to optimise our capabilities," he said.
When asked about the 92nd RMN celebration on April 27, Adly confirmed it would go ahead despite cost-cutting measures.
"Most adjustments, as mentioned by the prime minister, involve celebratory programmes, particularly Aidilfitri events.
"Operations, training, and civic engagement programmes will continue, though we encourage prudent spending for major celebrations," he said.
He added that the ministry will evaluate each programme while maintaining the objectives outlined in the Defence White Paper.
"While we must implement savings in certain areas, discussions are ongoing to identify where expenditure can be reduced without affecting operations or training," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor offers full scholarships for SPM top scorers to world's top 20 universities [WATCH]
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI: The Johor government has opened applications for its flagship youth scholarship programme offering full sponsorship for top-performing SPM students to pursue degrees at the world's leading universities.
State Unity, Heritage and Culture Committee chairman K. Raven Kumar said the Johor Orang Muda Scholarship (BOMJ) 2026, administered by
Yayasan Pelajaran Johor,
aims to nurture a new generation of global talent from the state.
He said the programme targets students who achieved outstanding results in the 2025/2026 Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia examination and aspire to further their studies at the world's top 20 universities.
"This scholarship provides comprehensive financial support, including a pre-university A-Level preparation programme in the country before students continue their studies abroad," he said.
Raven said priority would be given to applicants pursuing fields aligned with Johor's strategic development needs, to ensure graduates contribute to the state's future growth.
He urged eligible youths to seize the opportunity, describing it as a "gateway for Johor students to compete on the global stage while remaining rooted in the state's aspirations."
The BOMJ initiative reflects Johor's continued investment in human capital development, with the state seeking to produce highly skilled graduates capable of driving innovation and economic expansion.
Applications are now open, and candidates are encouraged to apply through Yayasan Pelajaran Johor.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Air India CEO Campbell Wilson resigns amid losses and regulatory scrutiny, source says
Article:
NEW DELHI: Air India chief executive officer (CEO) Campbell Wilson has resigned, a source with direct knowledge of the matter said on Tuesday, as the airline grapples with persistent losses and heightened regulatory scrutiny following a crash last year that killed 260 people.
The news of his resignation comes just days after its bigger rival IndiGo tapped aviation industry veteran Willie Walsh as its next CEO, as the country's two largest carriers come under pressure to tackle a mounting industry crisis stemming from the Middle East conflict compounded by domestic operational challenges.
Reuters reported in January that Air India's board was scouting for a new CEO to replace Wilson, a former Singapore Airlines veteran brought in to steer the Indian carrier's turnaround in 2022 after years of decline under government ownership.
The airline, which is contending with aircraft delivery delays, has also been reprimanded by regulators for safety lapses, including flying an aircraft eight times without an airworthiness certificate and running planes without checking emergency equipment.
New Zealand-born Wilson's term was due to end in 2027. He is currently serving a six-month notice period and plans to stay with the company until a successor is found, the source said, declining to be identified as they were not authorised to speak with media.
Air India did not immediately respond to Reuters' request for comment outside regular business hours. Wilson's resignation was reported by Indian publication Mint late on Monday.
Since taking over the top job in 2022, Wilson has steered the airline through the early and difficult stages of its turnaround, including overhauling Air India's engineering department and refurbishing planes amid supply chain disruptions.
"Over the last four years, Campbell did a good job in very tough circumstances," said Brendan Sobie, a Singapore-based independent aviation analyst.
"Finding the right candidate to complete (Air India's) transformation will not be easy and Tata will particularly feel the pressure to get this right following IndiGo's recent appointment of Willie Walsh," he said.
In December, Air India admitted there was a "need for urgent improvements in process discipline, communication, and compliance culture," Reuters reported.
Air India has a fleet of 191 planes and has placed orders for more than 500 aircraft.
It has lost money since being bought by Tata Group in 2022, with the financial pressure worsening since Pakistan banned Indian carriers from its airspace last year. Air India and its low-cost carrier Air India Express reported a combined loss of 98.08 billion rupees (US$1.05 billion) in the 2024-2025 financial year.
A prolonged Iran war will add further pressure on Air India's lucrative western routes, already scaled back due to Pakistan's restrictions.
Air India is chaired by N. Chandrasekaran, who is also the chair of Tata Group. Singapore Airlines holds a 25 per cent stake in Air India. (US$1 = 93.0600 Indian rupees)
(Reporting by Kanjyik Ghosh, Abhijith Ganapavaram, Abinaya Vijayaraghavan; Writing by Chris Thomas; Editing by David Gaffen, Himani Sarkar and Jamie Freed)
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Four siblings draw attention with unique names inspired by brands, icons
Article:
PETALING JAYA: Four siblings from Gombak near here are drawing attention for their distinctive names inspired by luxury brands, natural phenomena and global sports icons.
The third of the four, Hajarr Ferarie Mohd Zaki, 20, said she and her siblings – M'Glazzia Zidannial, 25, Shayenn Qistina, 22, and Emell Lebronz, 19 – were each given unconventional names based on their father's creative ideas.
The Universiti Teknologi Mara student from the Alor Gajah campus in Melaka said their father appreciates uniqueness and keeps up with trends, including when naming his children.
"Our father always follows global trends and enjoys things that are visually appealing and modern.
"He used to be a rocker, so it is not surprising that his preferences lean towards the trendy," she said.
Hajarr Ferarie said each name carries its own meaning. Her eldest brother's name, M'Glazzia Zidannial, was derived from the word "glacier" and football legend Zinedine Zidane.
"Shayenn was inspired by the Porsche Cayenne, adapted into Malay, while my name, Ferarie, comes from Ferrari.
"My younger brother Emell's is derived from 'e-mail', while Lebronz is inspired by the basketball player LeBron James," she said.
She said public reactions are often of surprise, with some finding the names amusing. She also nearly faced documentation issues as a child due to gender confusion in her MyKid, where her name was almost registered with a 'bin' despite her being female.
"From primary school until now, I have always drawn attention. When teachers or lecturers call my name, everyone turns to look.
"Sometimes it can be embarrassing, but at times it is amusing," she said.
Hajarr Ferarie said her feelings about her unique name have changed over time, noting both positive and negative effects on her daily life.
"On the positive side, people remember me easily and are always curious about the story behind my name. But on the negative side, many find it difficult to pronounce and sometimes misspell it in official matters.
"When I was younger, I wanted to change my name and have something more conventional," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Suspected Malayan tiger footprints found in Kluang, Perhilitan steps up patrols
Article:
KLUANG: Wildlife and National Parks Department (Perhilitan) officers were placed on high alert after suspected tiger paw prints were discovered at a modern agricultural project here.
The prints raised concerns over the presence of the endangered predator beyond its usual forest range.
Perhilitan director-general Datuk Abdul Kadir Abu Hashim said the department received a report on the discovery at 7.30pm on April 3.
A four-member team from the Batu Pahat and Kluang district Perhilitan offices confirmed the footprints were believed to belong to a Malayan tiger, he said in a statement today.
The tracks, estimated to be three to four days old, measured about 12cm in width and 11cm in length, consistent with a sub-adult or adult tiger.
The site lies about 15km from the Renggam Forest Reserve and 27km from the Kluang Forest Reserve, suggesting the animal may have strayed beyond its primary habitat.
Perhilitan said patrols and monitoring operations will be intensified for 14 days to ensure public safety in the surrounding area.
The Malayan Tiger Conservation unit will also deploy camera traps to track the animal's movement patterns and determine appropriate follow-up action.
Residents and workers in the area have been urged to remain vigilant, avoid venturing alone into plantations or forest fringes, and limit outdoor activity during early morning and late evening hours.
Abdul Kadir also called on the public to act as the department's "eyes and ears" by reporting sightings or sending information via its hotline or official complaint system.
The Malayan tiger, a critically endangered species, has increasingly been sighted near human settlements in recent years, often attributed to habitat encroachment and dwindling prey sources.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar: Fuel prices may rise, but Malaysia unlikely to face oil crisis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is unlikely to face an oil supply crisis in the coming months despite global uncertainties, although fuel prices may rise slightly.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said this is due to Petronas' strong track record as a net oil importer and the company's excellent relationships with major oil-producing countries.
"We are also net importers because our products are premium-branded and slightly higher priced, yet we still import.
"Praise must be given to Petronas for their excellent performance and good relations with oil-producing countries.
"Therefore, we are not facing a supply crisis. There may be some price fluctuations, but in the next few months, supply is relatively secure," he said.
Anwar made the remarks during a discussion at the 2026 Kuala Lumpur–Ankara Dialogue today.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ekuinas takes strategic stake in Ain Medicare via RM1bil fund
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Ekuiti Nasional Bhd (Ekuinas) has made a strategic investment in Ain Medicare Sdn Bhd, a Bumiputera-owned manufacturer of sterile parenteral pharmaceutical products.
The investment, made through Ekuinas' RM1 billion Tranche IV fund, will see the fund acquire a minority stake in Ain Medicare.
Ekuinas said the move supports its mandate to grow Bumiputera enterprises in strategic sectors that enhance Malaysia's long-term economic resilience.
Chief executive officer Aliff Omar Mohamad Omar said recognising healthcare as a priority industry for Ekuinas, the pharmaceuticals subsector plays a key role in higher-value industrial growth and national resilience.
He added that Ain Medicare has established itself as a homegrown leader in sterile parenteral products.
It has a 30-year track record, diversified customer base and strong research and development (R&D) pipeline.
"With the country's focus and advances in the pharmaceutical industry, it is increasingly recognised as a strategic sector that contributes to economic resilience, supply security and higher-value industrial development.
"Institutional capital has an important role in supporting the growth of scalable local players within priority industries.
"The sector's long-term fundamentals, underpinned by regulatory strengthening, export expansion and innovation in specialised products, present sustainable growth opportunities while contributing meaningfully to Malaysia's broader economic aspirations," he said in a statement.
Ain Medicare chairman Datuk Wan Ariff Wan Hamzah said the partnership with Ekuinas marks an important milestone.
He added that with Ekuinas' capital and institutional expertise, the company is poised to accelerate its strategic growth plans.
"We are focused on specialised generics and advanced injectables to tap into the growing Small Volume Injectable segment, as well as widening our international footprint."
Founded in 1993 and headquartered in Kota Bharu, Kelantan, Ain Medicare specialises in sterile parenteral pharmaceutical products.
Its portfolio spans intravenous solutions, small volume injectables, irrigation solutions and haemodialysis concentrates, serving hospitalised and dialysis patients as well as other critical care needs.
The company operates seven manufacturing facilities in Kota Bharu and Kulim, with certifications from bodies such as the National Pharmaceutical Regulatory Agency, Medical Device Authority and the International Organisation for Standardisation.
Ain Medicare has also prioritised Halal-certified products with in-house brands certified by Jabatan Kemajuan Islam Malaysia.
Its products are currently exported to 17 international markets across the Middle East, Asia Pacific, Europe, South America and Oceania.
The company also supports over 100 small and medium enterprises across logistics, packaging, engineering and technical services through its vendor development initiatives.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: East Coast tour bus operators face shutdown over diesel price hike
Article:
KOTA BARU: About 1,000 tour bus operators on the East Coast not in the list of diesel subsidy recipients under the Subsidised Diesel Control System (SKDS) risk being shut down following the rise in diesel prices.
The increase in diesel prices to RM6.02 per litre between April 2 and 8 has led to numerous booking cancellations, as rental rates rose by between 30 and 40 per cent.
Deputy chairman of the East Coast Bus Association (Pebat), Wan Mohd Nuhairi Wan Hamat, said tour bus operators do not fall under the category of companies eligible for SKDS, as the subsidy is limited to larger tourism firms.
He said operators have been forced to absorb higher diesel costs to continue operations.
"SKDS is only given to large tourism agencies with steady income, such as companies managing haj and umrah packages or international tourists.
"Tour bus operators are local service providers in the states, and we do not receive SKDS. The situation has become more difficult as many clients have cancelled bookings after fares were raised by 30 to 40 per cent following the diesel price hike.
"For example, previously, the rental for a return trip from Kota Bharu to Kuala Lumpur was RM3,000. Since the diesel price increase, we have had to raise the rental to as much as RM5,000, and many have cancelled their bookings," he said at a press conference after the East Coast Bus Operators and Drivers Assembly here yesterday.
A total of 50 tour bus operators from Kelantan, Terengganu and Pahang gathered to demand diesel subsidies, while urging Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, as well as the Tourism, Arts and Culture Ministry and the Finance Ministry, to intervene.
Wan Mohd Nuhairi said if diesel prices continue to rise without subsidy, operators on the East Coast may only last another two months.
"Previously, operators received fewer bookings during the monsoon season in November and December until Ramadan. We also did not receive bookings in January and February, as budgets for most government sectors, especially schools, had yet to be approved.
"We are open to discussions and are willing to comply with any conditions set by the government, as long as assistance is provided to ensure the sustainability of the industry and the welfare of workers. We hope the government will consider diesel subsidies at rates of RM1.88 or RM2.15, similar to other SKDS recipients," he said.
Sri Gunong Travel Sdn Bhd owner Wan Nurul Hakimah Wan Hamat said her family-run company, operating since 1978, has been under significant pressure, ever since the Covid-19 pandemic.
"A total of 15 long-distance charter trips have been cancelled over the past month.
"Our company has 10 buses, and four are still under monthly hire purchase. If this continues, it will be difficult for us to service the instalments as we have to absorb the higher diesel costs, compounded by cancellations," she said.
Tour bus driver Mohd Azuan Hamid, 45, said the situation could leave him without income due to the lack of bookings in recent weeks.
"When trips are cancelled, my income and daily activities are affected. Previously, I could earn RM300 for a return trip to Kuala Lumpur.
"I also depend on bus operators. If they have bookings, then I have work. I do not have a fixed salary like express bus drivers," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MOE takes responsibility for kayaking incident that killed 2 trainee teachers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek says the Education Ministry (MOE) will
take full responsibility
for the kayaking incident that claimed the lives of two trainee teachers during an off-campus programme.
Fadhlina visited Sultanah Bahiyah Hospital in Alor Star to pay her last respects to the victims, identified as Christopher Ling Jia Siang and Joey Ling Lin Siang.
Both were students at the Institut Pendidikan Guru (IPG) Perlis campus.
During her visit on Monday, she met the victims' families to personally convey the ministry's condolences.
"The ministry takes full responsibility for the accident. Justice must be upheld for the families," she said, adding that an investigation is underway.
As an immediate measure, several officers directly involved in organising the programme have been temporarily reassigned to the Perlis State Education Department pending the outcome of the investigation.
The ministry is also conducting a review of standard operating procedures (SOPs) for all off-campus activities across its institutions to strengthen safety measures.
On April 4, two trainee teachers, aged 20 and 21, drowned at Pantai Merdeka in Kedah after their kayaks were overwhelmed by strong currents.
Their bodies were later recovered by authorities, and the case was initially classified as sudden death.
The Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) said it
had not received prior notification
of the kayaking activity.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar: Global supply risks force urgent energy strategy overhaul
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia must urgently shift to a more sustainable, diversified and cost-effective energy strategy to safeguard long-term security, as global supply disruptions pose growing risks.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said the move is not merely an environmental measure, but a critical strategic step to ensure national resilience.
"For too long, we assumed the foundations of the global economy were resilient by default. That assumption has now been decisively challenged.
"When tensions rise in a region as strategically vital as the Gulf, the consequences extend far beyond the immediate theatre of conflict.
"In the immediate term, the government has acted to manage exposure to volatility, balancing supply needs with the imperative of mitigating cost-of-living pressures," he said.
He made the remarks in his keynote address at the Kuala Lumpur–Ankara Dialogue 2026 today.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tugboats caught operating illegally in Johor waters
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: Two tugboats attempting to slip into Malaysian waters without authorisation were detained by the Johor Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) Johor in separate operations off the state's east coast on Sunday.
Acting Johor MMEA director Maritime Captain Kama Azri Kamil said the arrests were made during coordinated patrols under multiple operations.
The first tugboat was intercepted at 12.30pm, some 16.5 nautical miles northeast of Tanjung Punggai here, while a second vessel was detained later the same day at 6.50pm, approximately 3.1 nautical miles southeast of Tanjung Penyusop, also in Kota Tinggi.
Both vessels were found operating within Malaysian territorial waters, placing them under the jurisdiction of local maritime enforcement authorities.
"Initial inspections revealed that both tugboats failed to produce valid permits to enter or operate in Malaysian waters, as required by the Marine Department," Kama Azri said in a statement today.
He added that the tugboats were suspected of breaching Section 491B(1)(l) of the Merchant Shipping Ordinance 1952 for entering national waters without proper authorisation.
Both tugboats have since been issued detention orders and the case has been handed over to the Tanjung Sedili Maritime Zone for further action.
Kama Azri said that enforcement and patrols would be intensified to safeguard the country's maritime sovereignty.
"All activities at sea are under close watch and firm action will be taken against those who violate the law," he said, telling the public to report suspicious maritime activities via emergency lines or the Johor Maritime operations centre.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Authorities struggle as crypto drives new wave of money laundering
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Rapid advances in digital technology are opening new avenues for financial crime, with illicit funds increasingly channelled through cryptocurrencies.
The decentralised and online nature of these assets makes them difficult to monitor and trace using conventional methods, posing a growing challenge to enforcement agencies.
The situation is further complicated by the pace of cryptocurrency technology, which continues to outstrip legislative and regulatory developments.
This has left enforcement agencies struggling to keep up, often reacting rather than implementing pre-emptive measures.
In Malaysia, authorities have acknowledged cases involving the use of cryptocurrencies for money laundering, although the number of cases and value of assets involved have not been disclosed.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said cryptocurrency transactions, conducted entirely in the virtual space, require a different approach to detection compared with conventional financial transactions.
Without direct access to digital wallet information, tracing such transactions becomes significantly more complex.
He added that investigations still rely on "manual methods", including examining suspects' devices and gathering information through questioning to identify transaction details within the virtual environment.
The National Financial Crime Centre (NFCC) is also facing challenges in tracking money laundering activities involving cryptocurrencies.
Cross-border operations
NFCC director-general Datuk Seri Shamshun Baharin Mohd Jamil said many platforms and servers linked to virtual assets are based overseas, placing transaction data beyond the immediate reach of local authorities.
The use of anonymous wallets and technologies that obscure transaction trails further complicates efforts to identify the true owners of funds.
Decentralised finance techniques and cross-chain transfers also add layers of complexity to investigations.
He said the NFCC's role focuses on operational coordination, intelligence sharing and developing procedures related to digital assets, while investigative and enforcement powers lie with agencies such as the police, Securities Commission and Bank Negara Malaysia.
Bank Negara, through its virtual asset risk assessment, has highlighted a growing trend of money laundering risks linked to virtual assets, often associated with high-risk scam activities that exploit the speed of blockchain transactions.
Through blockchain, assets, data and information can be transferred in real time without the need for traditional intermediaries such as banks.
At the international level, misuse of virtual assets has raised concerns over the erosion of the global financial system's integrity.
Given the cross-border nature of cryptocurrency transactions, stronger legal cooperation between countries is essential.
Although cases involving cryptocurrency-linked money laundering have been detected, details on how such activities are carried out remain limited.
The rapidly evolving digital landscape calls for stronger technological capabilities, regulatory frameworks and international cooperation to ensure effective enforcement.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Customs seizes 35.6 tonnes of dried shark fins worth RM8.24 million
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Customs Department has seized 35.6 tonnes of dried shark fins worth RM8.24 million, believed to have been imported without valid permits and intended for sale to high-end restaurants in the country.
Selangor Customs director Mohamad Azhar Ahmad Paharaz said the seizure involved three containers intercepted at the North Port Free Zone in Port Klang during operations conducted on Feb 12, March 2 and March 9 under Ops Petasan.
He said the containers, comprising one 20-foot unit and two 40-foot units, were detained following intelligence from the Customs headquarters' narcotics branch.
"The containers were carrying dried shark fins weighing 35,660kg, believed to have been imported without valid permits.
"The total value of the goods is estimated at RM7.13 million, with duties and taxes amounting to RM1.11 million," he said at a press conference today.
Based on the seizure value, the shark fins are estimated to fetch around RM200 per kilogramme, underscoring their high commercial value in the premium food market.
Azhar said preliminary investigations found the shipments had arrived at Port Klang between Feb 3 and March 7, and originated from a country in South America.
He added that the importation of shark fins is subject to strict controls, including permits issued by the Malaysian Quarantine and Inspection Services Department (Maqis) as well as approvals under the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES) from the Fisheries Department.
"We found that the importer made false declarations in Customs Form 1 and the manifest, labelling the goods as 'dried fish skin' and 'dried foodstuff' to evade detection," he said.
The case is being investigated under Section 135(1)(a) of the Customs Act for importing prohibited goods and Section 133(1)(a) of the same Act for making false declarations.
Upon conviction, offenders may face fines of up to 20 times the value of the goods or imprisonment of up to five years, or both, for the former offence.
For false declarations, penalties include fines of up to RM500,000 or imprisonment of up to seven years, or both.
Azhar said investigations are ongoing together with the Fisheries Department and Maqis.
Members of the public with information can contact the Customs toll-free hotline at 1-800-88-8855 or the nearest Customs office.
Informants' identities will be kept confidential.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'I didn't understand': Cambodian man shocked after US deportation, jailed in Eswatini
Article:
PHNOM PENH: A Cambodian refugee long-settled in the United States, ex-convict Pheap Rom, remains bewildered at how he wound up behind bars in the African nation of Eswatini for months after being swept up in Donald Trump's deportation blitz.
When Rom and nine other men – shackled and escorted onto a plane by US authorities – landed in the kingdom of Eswatini in October, they were greeted on the tarmac by a squad of "military guys with guns and masks", the 43-year-old said.
"I didn't know what was going to happen," he told AFP in an interview in the Cambodian capital Phnom Penh, where he was repatriated in late March.
"I didn't understand why I was being deported to Africa because I'm Cambodian."
Rom is one of around 20 men the United States has deported to landlocked Eswatini – bordering South Africa and Mozambique – under a Trump administration scheme challenged in courts and described by rights advocates as akin to "human trafficking."
Eswatini, Africa's last absolute monarchy, is among several "third countries" accepting migrants under shadowy deals enabling the US president's push for mass deportations.
The nation formerly known as Swaziland agreed to accept up to 160 deportees in exchange for around US$5.1 million, with plans to forward them to their home countries after Washington said their direct repatriations were denied.
But Rom – living in the United States with "permanent resident" status since 1985 after his family fled Cambodia's genocidal Khmer Rouge regime – is just the second to be repatriated from Eswatini, after a Jamaican was returned home last year.
The remainder may still be trapped inside the deportation process, opaque even to those inside it.
- 'IN GOOD HANDS' -
Rom served a 15-year prison sentence in the United States after pleading guilty to attempted murder for firing a gun during two neighbourhood disputes, leaving several people wounded.
"I know what I did was wrong," he said. "I accepted my punishment."
He was detained by immigration authorities upon his release in November 2024, and his green card was revoked after an immigration judge ordered his deportation due to his felony conviction.
He expected to be sent to Cambodia. But landing in Eswatini was like turning back the clock to his prison term, Rom said.
His jailers seemed unaware that the men had served their time and looked at them as if they were "criminals because of what the (US) administration was portraying us to be."
For the first two months at the Matsapha Correctional Centre, Rom said he and fellow deportees "went through misery" – allowed outdoors for only 15 minutes a day and given one weekly phone call.
"We had an attorney there that was willing to try to come and talk to us, but they weren't letting that attorney in," he said.
Lawyers in Eswatini have corroborated his claim to AFP, saying they have been repeatedly denied access to people expelled by the United States who have been detained without charge.
Eswatini's government has said US deportees were "in good hands" and receiving healthcare, including counselling.
- 'UNIQUELY BARBARIC' -
Much of Trump's deportation campaign has been executed by US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), criticised for using heavy-handed tactics during crackdowns in US cities.
ICE did not reply to a request for comment about Rom's case.
In July, when the first deportees were sent to Eswatini, a US Department of Homeland Security spokeswoman said on social media they were "so uniquely barbaric that their home countries refused to take them back."
But Rom and his lawyer Tin Thanh Nguyen both expressed doubts the agency even tried to deport him directly to Cambodia, the only country where he has citizenship.
Nguyen is struggling to piece together the logic that led to his client being marooned in a country where he had no ties.
The lawyer painted a picture of a chaotic process, saying Rom was not interviewed in the United States to verify his nationality and was registered for deportation to Thailand, the country where he was born in a refugee camp.
"If ICE was requesting travel documents from Thailand, they went to the wrong country," Nguyen told AFP.
Chann Rotana, a spokesman for Cambodia's foreign ministry, told AFP that the United States had previously sent Cambodian deportees to the country directly with no issues.
"We also don't know why the US this time sent him to Eswatini," the spokesman said.
Now living in Phnom Penh after the Cambodian government facilitated his repatriation from Eswatini, Rom said he was still "getting used to the freedom" but was ready to move on.
Eventually, he hopes to find work, stay in touch with his relatives in the United States and start a new family in Cambodia, he said.
"I want to create a new memory here, because this is my second chance in life."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trailer driver charged with three counts of murder after Johor crash [WATCH]
Article:
SEGAMAT: A trailer driver was charged in the magistrate's court today with three counts of murder over a collision that killed three family members on April 2.
Muhamad Shafiq Salleh, 29, nodded to indicate he understood the charges read out by magistrate Amalina Johar.
No plea was recorded, as murder cases are heard in the High Court.
He is accused of murdering S. Palamiandy, 77; K. Myakrishnan, 72; and S. Sevendai, 62.
The offences allegedly took place at Km212 of Jalan Johor Baru-Seremban at 3.50pm on April 2.
The charges were framed under Section 302 of the Penal Code, which carries the death penalty or 30–40 years' imprisonment and at least 12 strokes of the cane if not sentenced to death.
Deputy Public Prosecutor Nur Aina Muhammad Ismail Tadj appeared for the prosecution, while the accused was unrepresented.
The prosecution requested a new date pending completion of post-mortem, medical, Puspakom, forensic and pathology reports.
Bail was denied and May 21 was fixed for mention.
The
three family members
died in a crash involving three vehicles near the Taman Golden Hill junction in Gemas Baru at 4pm last Thursday.
The accident occurred
about 200m from
the victims' home as they were returning from a relative's house in Batu Anam.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stubbed out: Two Singaporeans fined RM1,000 each for littering cigarette butts in JB
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU: Two Singaporean men were fined RM1,000 each and ordered to perform community service by the Sessions Court today for littering in a public place.
The accused, architect Lim Hu Shen and project manager Tan Boon Haw, both 38, pleaded guilty after the charges were read to them in Mandarin before judge Datuk Che Wan Zaidi Che Wan Ibrahim.
Che Wan Zaidi also ordered the two to perform six hours of community service, to be completed within six months.
The court further ruled that both men would face a month's jail if they failed to pay the fine.
According to the charge sheet, the men were caught throwing cigarette butts in a public area instead of into designated waste bins.
The offence was committed at the main entrance of KSL City Mall here between 11am and 11.11am on Jan 3.
The charge was framed under Section 77A(1) of the Solid Waste and Public Cleansing Management Act 2007 (Act 672), which allows for community service orders under Subsection 77B(1) of the same Act.
The section provides for a fine of up to RM2,000 and a maximum of 12 hours of community service upon conviction.
Solid Waste and Public Cleansing Management Corporation (SWCorp) prosecuting officer Rubiah Maulud appeared for the prosecution, while the accused were unrepresented.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ethical dilemma of AI warfare without human oversight
Article:
LETTER:
ARTIFICIAL intelligence has moved beyond a supporting role to become the central nervous system of modern conflict, compressing decision-making from hours to seconds.
This is not simply technological progress. It marks a structural shift in how wars are fought and how they are decided.
At the core of this transformation is speed.
AI systems process vast streams of battlefield data, from satellite imagery to drone feeds, and convert them into actionable decisions almost instantly.
This acceleration is already visible in contemporary conflicts. In 2006, the Israeli military identified about 300 targets in Lebanon.
By 2023, AI-enabled systems generated around 15,000 targets within just 35 days of operations in Gaza.
What once required weeks of human deliberation can now be done in real time, creating a battlefield that is faster and increasingly detached from human restraint.
The spread of low-cost, autonomous drones has shifted the balance away from expensive platforms towards scalable systems.
In 2024 alone, Ukraine produced around two million drones, fundamentally reshaping how wars are fought.
The most troubling consequence lies in the ethical implications. The rapid integration of AI into military systems has far outpaced the development of international norms.
Decisions involving life and death are increasingly influenced by probabilistic models.
Because algorithms operate on probabilities rather than certainty, the risk of misidentification grows alongside the speed of execution.
This risk is no longer theoretical. It was starkly illustrated on Feb 28, 2026, when a strike on the Shajareh Tayyebeh girls' school in Minab, Iran, reportedly killed 168 people, mostly children.
While human operators remain formally in the loop, the sheer volume and speed of AI-generated targets create what is known as automation bias.
As warfare becomes increasingly dependent on cloud computing, data centres are emerging as strategic targets.
This development blurs the boundary between military operations and civilian infrastructure, exposing societies to new forms of risk.
Without clear safeguards and meaningful human oversight, the rise of AI warfare risks ushering in an era defined by speed, opacity and reduced accountability as war is no longer shaped by generals, but by algorithms.
ZULKIFLI MUSA
Head of Technology and AI,
Universiti Sains Malaysia
*The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 4 policemen remanded in RM12,000 bribery probe by MACC [WATCH]
Article:
KOTA BARU: Four policemen were remanded for 5 days by the magistrate's court here today over a RM12,000 bribery case that took place last year.
The remand, issued by magistrate Rais Imran Hamid, runs until April 11.
The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) obtained the remand under Section 117 of the Criminal Procedure Code.
The suspects, clad in MACC's orange lock-up attire, arrived at the court compound at about 8.40am in a van.
The suspects, aged between 22 and 42, include two lance corporals, a corporal, and a sergeant.
They were detained by MACC at their offices yesterday afternoon.
All four, attached to Tanah Merah police station, are believed to have received the money in exchange for not acting against a man caught with prohibited items in his vehicle.
The man, a local, was caught late last year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cops arrest 2 men over alleged assault of woman in Penang
Article:
BALIK PULAU: Police have arrested two men over criminal intimidation, mischief, and causing hurt after a woman was injured in an incident on Jalan Tun Sardon on March 4.
Penang police chief Datuk Azizee Ismail said the investigation is being conducted under Sections 323, 506, and 427 of the Penal Code.
"The investigation paper will be referred to the deputy public prosecutor in the near future for further instructions," he said today.
The incident left a 40-year-old woman injured. She also received threats and had her vehicle damaged.
The incident is believed to have stemmed from a misunderstanding involving the suspects.
The victim lodged a report at the Balik Pulau police station and sought treatment at Balik Pulau Hospital.
Police from the Southwest District Criminal Investigation Department detained the suspects shortly after the report was filed.
Yesterday, Jelutong MP RSN Rayer raised concerns over the lack of prosecution in the
alleged assault case
, despite video evidence and a medical report confirming the victim's injuries.
He questioned why no charges had been brought, suggesting that the case could at least fall under Section 323 of the Penal Code for voluntarily causing hurt.
Rayer said police later informed them that no further action (NFA) would be taken and that the case had been referred to a magistrate.
LINK:
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: CIMB targets Asean wealth boom under Forward30 plan
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: CIMB Group Holdings Bhd is ramping up its push into Asean's fast-growing affluent and wealth segment.
It is positioning the move as a core driver of its Forward30 strategy to strengthen its deposit base and boost higher-value cross-selling opportunities.
The banking group is targeting a rapidly expanding market, with Asean's affluent population expected to grow 5.0-6.0 per cent annually.
This will account for as much as 65 per cent to 70 per cent of the region's total population by 2030.
At the core of CIMB's strategy is an advisory-led, insight-driven wealth proposition aimed at helping clients preserve and grow their assets.
To support this, CIMB has invested heavily in enhancing its wealth capabilities.
This includes deeper portfolio reviews, curated wealth events and market intelligence backed by a dedicated chief investment officer (CIO) alongside personalised advisory services delivered by relationship managers.
The group also offers a comprehensive suite of wealth management products across both conventional and Islamic banking.
It has introduced AI-enabled tools and propriety insights to empower its frontliners while customers are able to benefit from real-time portfolio tracking, access to CIO insights and tailored asset allocation as well as product recommendations.
CIMB said its regional footprint plays a crucial role in this expansion, with its operations in Singapore serving as a key Asean wealth hub, particularly for cross-border investment needs in the healthcare, retirement and education areas.
CIMB Group Consumer Banking chief executive officer Haniz Nazlan said the region's affluent segment is being driven by rising incomes, entrepreneurship and intergenerational wealth creation, resulting in increasingly sophisticated financial needs.
"Our ambition is to grow our wealth asset under management (AUM) two-fold and deliver stronger wealth and cross-sell revenue by 2030, enabling us to sustain a non-interest income (NOII) contribution between 33 per cent to 34 per cent,"
As at December 2025, the group reported solid growth in deposits and current account savings account (CASA) balances.
Total deposits rose 5.4 per cent year-on-year to RM524.4 billion and CASA balances increasing 1.6 per cent to RM224.1 billion, bringing the CASA ratio to 42.7 per cent, further reinforcing a stable funding base
The group is also expanding its regional wealth platform, launching CIMB Private Wealth in Indonesia with plans to roll out similar offerings in Malaysia and other markets within the year.
The group believes Asean's wealth management sector remains underpenetrated, presenting a compelling growth opportunity for regional banks with strong networks and advisory capabilities.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sunway, IJM mixed in early trade after takeover lapse
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sunway Bhd and IJM Corp Bhd show mixed early trade moves on Tuesday, following the lapse of the former's takeover offer.
Sunway opened at RM4.99 and traded between RM4.99 and RM5.03, essentially flat from yesterday's close.
Meanwhile, IJM opened at RM2.33, down 1.3 per cent from its RM2.36 close, moving between RM2.30 and RM2.34.
At the time of writing, Sunway shares were up one sen or 0.20 per cent to RM5.00, giving it a market capitalisation of RM34.03 billion, while IJM shares were down seven sen or 2.97 per cent to RM2.29, with a market capitalisation of RM8.35 billion.
Trading was moderate, with IJM changing hands about 2.00 million shares and Sunway about 1.02 million shares.
Yesterday, Sunway's RM11 billion takeover bid for IJM fell short, receiving only 33.43 per cent acceptance by the April 6 deadline, well below the majority needed.
The offer, launched in January, included 10 per cent cash at 31.5 sen per IJM share and 90 per cent in new Sunway shares valued at RM5.65 each, a 28 per cent premium over IJM's market price at the time.
If successful, the deal would have required issuing about 1.76 billion new Sunway shares and RM1.1 billion in cash.
The lack of shareholder support meant the takeover could not proceed.
IJM said it will continue executing its strategy to deliver value across its portfolio.
Its group chief executive officer and managing director Datuk Lee Chun Fai said the board respects the decision of its shareholders and the confidence they have placed in the company's long-term intrinsic value.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Second Filipino killed in Middle East war after missile hits home in Israel
Article:
MANILA: The Middle East war has claimed its second Philippine victim, Manila said Tuesday, when a missile struck the home of a Filipina living in Israel.
The woman was killed in the port city of Haifa on Sunday "alongside her Israeli husband and elderly parents-in-law", the foreign affairs department said, without naming the victims.
Israeli rescue services said Monday that the bodies of four people had been recovered from the rubble of a residential building in the city, after it was struck by an Iranian missile the previous day.
Israeli news outlets identified the Filipina victim's given name as Lucille-Jean, saying that she and her family had been pulled from the rubble of their collapsed residence following an hours-long rescue effort.
"The Philippine Embassy in Tel Aviv has informed the family and is providing all necessary assistance, including arrangements for the earliest possible repatriation of her remains despite the current travel situation in the region," the foreign affairs department said Tuesday.
Mary Ann Velasquez De Vera, a 32-year-old caregiver, became the war's first Philippine fatality on March 1 as she attempted to escort her elderly ward to an Israeli bomb shelter.
At least two million Filipinos who live in the Middle East have found themselves in the crossfire since the United States and Israel launched their attack on Iran on February 28.
Mostly guestworkers, the overseas Filipino worker community in the region sends billions of dollars in remittances home each year to families that depend on them as the primary breadwinners.
Thousands of Filipino sailors, meanwhile, have found themselves effectively trapped in the Strait of Hormuz, awaiting safe passage through the now-deadly shipping lane, which Iran has effectively bottlenecked.
The Philippines said last week that Iran had pledged to allow "the safe, unhindered, and expeditious passage through the Strait of Hormuz of Philippine-flagged vessels, energy sources, and all Filipino seafarers", though the timeline was unclear.
US President Donald Trump doubled down on Monday on a threat to wreck Iran's civilian infrastructure, warning that US forces could destroy bridge and power plants in the country and that a truce proposal from international mediators was not yet enough.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Taiwan opposition leader makes rare visit to China
Article:
TAIPEI: Taiwan's main opposition leader will travel to China on Tuesday for a rare visit aimed at building cross-strait "peace", but the government warns Beijing will seek to stop US arms sales to the democratic island.
Kuomintang (KMT) chairwoman Cheng Li-wun, who will become the party's first leader to visit China in a decade, has insisted on meeting Chinese President Xi Jinping before she visits the United States – Taiwan's main security backer.
The KMT supports closer relations with China, which claims Taiwan is part of its territory and has threatened to use force to seize it.
But Cheng, whose unexpected rise to the top of the KMT drew a congratulatory message from Xi in October, has been accused by critics, including inside the party, of being too pro-China.
Before departing with a delegation for Shanghai, Cheng told journalists that Taiwan "must do everything in our power to prevent war from breaking out."
"To preserve peace is to preserve Taiwan," Cheng told a press conference at KMT headquarters in Taipei.
"Goodwill must be built up and mutual trust needs to be expanded, step by step, by both sides."
Ahead of the trip, Taiwan's top China policy body warned Beijing would attempt to "cut off Taiwan's military purchases from the US and cooperation with other countries", which the KMT denies.
"This trip is entirely for cross-strait peace and stability, so it has nothing to do with arms procurement or other issues," Cheng said last week.
Taiwanese lawmakers have been at loggerheads over the government's plan to spend NT$1.25 trillion (US$39 billion) on defence, which has been stalled for months in the opposition-controlled parliament.
Cheng will be in China for six days, visiting Shanghai, Nanjing and Beijing where she hopes to meet Xi.
While KMT party members regularly fly to China for exchanges with officials, its last leader to visit was Hung Hsiu-chu in 2016.
- US PRESSURE -
China severed high-level contact with Taiwan that year after Tsai Ing-wen of the Democratic Progressive Party won the presidency and rejected Beijing's claims on the island.
Cross-strait relations have worsened since then as China ramped up military pressure with near daily deployments of fighter jets and warships near Taiwan and regular large-scale military drills.
Cheng's trip to China comes a month before US President Donald Trump is scheduled to visit Beijing for a summit with Xi.
The United States has been piling pressure on Taiwanese opposition lawmakers to back a proposal for defence purchases, including US weapons, to deter a potential Chinese attack.
Cheng has railed against the government's proposal, insisting "Taiwan isn't an ATM" and instead backed a KMT plan to allocate NT$380 billion for US weapons with the option for more acquisitions.
But she faces deepening divisions inside her party over how to counter China's military threats, with more moderate senior figures in the party pushing for a much higher budget.
While the United States has long been ambiguous about its willingness to defend Taiwan, Washington remains Taipei's biggest arms supplier, which angers Beijing.
The United States approved the sale of US$11 billion worth of arms to Taiwan in December. More deals are in the pipeline, but there have been doubts about whether they would proceed after Xi warned Trump against sending weapons to Taiwan.
Cheng has insisted she supports Taiwan having a strong defence, but said the island does not have to choose between Beijing and Washington.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Global uncertainties test resilience of Malaysia's halal industry, says Zahid
Article:
TASEK GELUGOR: Global uncertainties are increasingly shaping the trajectory of the halal industry, with geopolitical developments now exerting direct pressure on supply chains and production costs.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said the global halal sector today faces not only market challenges but also the ripple effects of international conflicts, particularly in the Middle East involving key strategic routes such as the Strait of Hormuz.
"These disruptions have significantly impacted global supply chains," he said, noting that the effects extend beyond the energy sector and into the broader halal ecosystem.
Malaysia is widely recognised as one of the world's most developed Islamic finance jurisdictions and a pioneer in halal governance.
The national halal food sector holds significant growth potential, with the global halal market currently valued at over RM16.02 trillion and projected to expand to RM22.88 trillion by 2030.
Malaysia is home to some 1.2 million micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs).
Speaking at the groundbreaking of the Perda Halal Park Industry development project here today, Zahid said the global conflict has triggered a domino effect across the industry, from rising logistics costs and shipment delays to uncertainties in market access.
"Halal products, especially fresh food and temperature-controlled goods, are highly dependent on the efficiency and stability of global supply chains.
"Rising energy and raw material prices are putting additional pressure on production costs," he added.
He said in some sectors, fertiliser alone accounted for up to 60 per cent of production costs, directly affecting the entire halal food value chain in the country.
"Despite these challenges, the current global climate presents an opportunity for Malaysia to strengthen its position as a stable, trusted and resilient halal hub," he said.
Zahid said efforts to diversify export markets, reinforce domestic supply chains and improve logistics efficiency was crucial in ensuring the industry's continued growth amid global headwinds.
"This is the time for us to move away from over-reliance on global systems and build stronger, more organised and resilient domestic capabilities," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Vietnam parliament elects party chief To Lam as president
Article:
HANOI: Vietnam Communist Party boss To Lam was elected president by the National Assembly on Tuesday, capping his bid to centralise authority in a nation where senior cadres have traditionally governed collectively.
After securing another term as the party's general secretary in January, Lam will now take over the number two position in Vietnamese politics – unifying leadership of the party and state as President Xi Jinping did in neighbouring China.
"For me, taking the responsibility of the general secretary and the president is a very huge honor...a responsibility, and a sacred and noble duty," Lam said in a speech after being sworn in.
Tran Thanh Man, chairman of the National Assembly, said 100 per cent of deputies who were present approved the resolution electing Lam president for the 2026-2031 term.
Lam is the first person to secure the top two jobs through the party's normal leadership selection processes, rather than stepping in following the death of an officeholder.
In less than two years as party chief, he has swept aside rivals and transformed the country through an aggressive reform drive – literally redrawing the map as he combined provinces and slashed bureaucracy.
Lam has set an ambitious target of 10 per cent annual growth for the Southeast Asian manufacturing hub and muscled the party behind his vision for development-oriented reform.
Vietnam is both a repressive one-party state and a regional economic bright spot, where the Communist Party has sought to deliver rapid growth to bolster its legitimacy.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar to urge Asean, China to fast-track South China Sea Code of Conduct at May summit
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 7 — Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today said he will urge his regional counterparts and China to expedite the conclusion of Code of Conduct (COC) on the South China Sea at the Asean Leaders’ Summit in Philippines next month.
Barring some recent tensions between China and the Philippines, Anwar said the contested waters remain relatively calm despite attempts to project the situation otherwise, particularly by the West.
“Neither us (Asean) nor China have shown any sort of antagonism, animosity, or for that matter, any intention to escalate the tensions.
“We have action relations in trade, commerce, military, and in every field possible,” he said at the inaugural Kuala Lumpur-Ankara Dialogue 2026, here, today.
“So, whether it is the Indian Ocean or the South China Sea, we (Asean) maintain our right and our fierce independence in attempting to resolve our problems and differences.
During the 2025 Asean Summit in Kuala Lumpur last October, Anwar said Chinese Premier Li Qiang has agreed to expedite talks on the COC.
Asean and China signed the non-binding Declaration on the Conduct of Asean and China (DOC) in 2002 and initiated talks for a legally-binding COC in 2013.
Both parties reportedly completed the second reading of the COC draft in 2023.

Title: Penang plans new state administrative centre, legislative assembly on Silicon Island
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, April 7 — The Penang government is planning the construction of a new state administrative centre including a new state legislative assembly on Silicon Island.
Penang Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow said the Penang Development Corporation (PDC) was appointed to assess spatial and design requirements from various state agencies.
He said data collection is underway to enable detailed architectural and land use planning for the administrative centre.
“The planning process has started, including gathering requirements from agencies on the space they need,” he told reporters in a press conference at the official launch of publicity and public display of the final Penang Silicon Island local plan 2050 (RTPSI) at the Amari Spice here.
He said PDC will proceed with architectural and land use design once they’ve gathered the relevant information.
Chow said the site allocated for the administrative centre is limited to about 2.8 hectares (seven acres) and will be subject to strict development controls.
“It will not be a large, centralised complex. Existing government offices will largely remain in Komtar, so only some may relocate to the new site,” he said.
When asked if it will be similar to Putrajaya, he dismissed it while saying it will be Penang’s own model.
He also confirmed that there are long-term plans to relocate the state legislative assembly to Silicon Island as part of the administrative hub.
“Since the land for the administrative centre is expected to be ready by 2028, construction can start after that,” he said.

Title: Former Johor property executive pleads not guilty to RM1m CBT charges
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, April 7 —A former sales executive officer of a property company claimed trial in the Sessions Court here today to five counts of criminal breach of trust involving more than RM1 million.
Rafidah Abdul Hamid, 48, pleaded not guilty after the charges were read out before Judge Datuk Che Wan Zaidi Che Wan Ibrahim.
According to the charge sheet, the accused is charged with committing criminal breach of trust by fraudulently using money, amounting to between RM73,000 and RM317,610, for her own benefit.
The accused, who is a single mother of three children, is charged with committing the act on the first floor of a building in Jalan Ibrahim here between May 30, 2022 and October 15 last year.
She was charged under Section 408 of the Penal Code for criminal breach of trust, which provides for a one-year to 14-year jail term with whipping and a fine upon conviction.
The prosecution was conducted by Deputy Public Prosecutor Siti Fatimah Mamu. while the accused unrepresented.
Siti Fatimah  proposed to the court a bail of RM8,000 for each charge, with one surety and the surrender of the accused’s international passport.
Che Wan Zaidi then set a RM40,000 bail for all charges, with the additional conditions of a single surety for the accused, the surrender of her international passport and reporting to the nearest police station every month. In addition, the accused is also refrained from harassing witnesses.
The court then set May 6 for mention.
Based on the facts of the case, a manager of the company involved filed a police report on March 31 after receiving a call from a law firm regarding a property sale and purchase agreement involving two individuals.
The company’s internal review found that there were no official records regarding the sale and purchase transaction recorded in its system or accounts.
An internal probe also found that the accused, who has been serving in her role since 2021, was believed to have received an undisclosed sum of money from a buyer in January 2024. However, the money was not handed over to the accounts department or deposited into the company’s official account.
The accused is also alleged to have forged the signatures of the company officers in the sale and purchase agreement documents prepared by the law firm as a means to complete the transaction.
In addition, the accused is also believed to have used the same ruse to receive money from another individual in the same year.
The company was only made aware of the matter after receiving inquiries from the legal department before a police report was lodged for further action.

Title: Land for Silicon Island LRT depot set for handover in May, says Penang CM
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, April 7 — Land for the Light Rail Transit (LRT) depot on Silicon Island is expected to be handed over to MRT Corporation Sdn Bhd (MRTC) as early as May this year, said Penang Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow.
He said the land for the depot, measuring about 27.7 hectares (68.5 acres), will be transferred over to MRTC once complete.
He said development can proceed in phases, without waiting for the full 930 ha (2,300 acres) of reclamation to be completed.
“We will not wait for the entire reclamation to be finished before starting topside development,” he said.
“Once the land is ready, construction can begin, starting with the LRT depot site,” he added during a press conference at the official launch of publicity and public viewing in the final Penang Silicon Island local plan 2050 (RTPSI) at the Amari Spice here.
Chow said Silicon Island represents more than just a land reclamation project but a long-term commitment by the Penang government to future generations.
An artist’s impression of Penang Silicon Island. — Picture by Opalyn Mok
Chow said the project aims to position Penang as a “global gateway” while addressing the state’s industrial land shortage through the creation of 930 ha of new land.
Through the proposed Green Tech Park, he said the state will offer a plug-and-play ecosystem tailored to meet strict environmental, social and governance (ESG) requirements of global corporations.
He added that the initiative is projected to contribute up to RM1.1 trillion to Malaysia’s gross domestic product by 2050.
It is also projected to create 220,000 high-quality jobs across sectors ranging from high-tech engineering to the creative and services economy.
“This is inclusive development, where prosperity is shared across all levels of society,” he said.
Chow noted that RTPSI 2050 is Malaysia’s first local plan designed specifically for a fully reclaimed greenfield site, allowing planners to work without legacy infrastructure constraints.
Land for the Light Rail Transit (LRT) depot on Silicon Island is expected to be handed over to MRT Corporation Sdn Bhd (MRTC) as early as May this year, said Penang Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow. — Picture by Opalyn Mok
He said the plan incorporates GeoBIM, integrating Geographic Information Systems and Building Information Modelling, to enhance urban governance and provide investors with fast, transparent and highly accurate digital planning data.
On sustainability targets, Chow said the plan includes a 45 per cent reduction in carbon emissions, full reliance on renewable energy in the Green Tech Park, and a 70:30 modal shift in favour of public transport, cycling and pedestrian networks.
He said a “Heart of the Island” (HOTI) concept will serve as a vibrant and iconic urban centre.
There will also be policies such as a no-dig utility framework and dedicated utility corridors to improve infrastructure management and road quality.
Chow said the public display period for the plan will be available until May 8.
He called on stakeholders, including the private sector, NGOs, academics and the public, to contribute their feedback.
“We want the people of Penang to be co-creators of Silicon Island, ensuring that every aspect of its development reflects public aspirations and remains resilient for the next 50 years,” he said.

Title: Anwar: Malaysia’s fuel supply secure until June, thanks Iranian president for safe transit of ships via Hormuz
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 7 — Malaysia has sufficient fuel supply to meet local consumption needs until June, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim reiterated today.
Speaking at the Kuala Lumpur-Ankara Dialogue 2026, Anwar credited national oil giant Petronas for building robust ties with other oil producing nations that has ensured adequate fuel supply for Malaysia despite the ongoing war in West Asia.
“Although Malaysia is an oil producing nation, we are also a net importer because our products are of premium quality.
“But, our supply is adequate for the next few months,” he explained, after his keynote address at the Grand Hyatt Kuala Lumpur, here, today.
Similarly, Anwar also assured that Malaysia has adequate supply of liquified natural gas (LNG), owing to the country’s massive domestic production, long-standing import ties with Australia as well as Petronas’ successful partnership with the Canadian energy sector.
Anwar also expressed his gratitude to Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian for promising the safe passage of Malaysian ships carrying crude oil across the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran has tightened control over the crucial maritime route since the war began nearly 40 days ago, with the United States (US) threathening tougher military actions if the former refuses to reopen the waterway.
The first Malaysian ship safely sailed through the Strait of Hormuz yesterday, after Anwar secured safe passage for seven Malaysian vessels during his recent call with Pezeshkian.

Title: UPSI bowling centre employee found dead had complained of headache, says manager
Article:
IPOH, April 7 — An employee at a bowling centre in Tanjong Malim had complained to his wife about having a headache before being found dead in a storage room at the premises early this morning.
Universiti Pendidikan Sultan Idris (UPSI) Sports Bowl manager Dzulkarnain Sapian said the employee was only found around 1am after his wife came to the bowling centre to look for her husband, who had not returned home after finishing work last night.
Dzulkarnain said he, together with the victim’s wife and another staff member, had opened the storeroom door, which is usually not locked, but initially had difficulty doing so because the victim’s body was behind the door.
“The victim had complained to his wife last evening about having a headache. That’s all that I know,” Dzulkarnain said when contacted here today.
Dzulkarnain said the victim, Mohd Radzi Ariffin, 53, was a cleaner at the private company, UPSI Sports Bowl, which operates within the UPSI campus.
The funeral prayers are scheduled to be held at the Al-Mursyidin Mosque UPSI Mosque after the post-mortem by the authorities at the Slim Hospital is completed, following which the burial will take place at the Taman Bernam Cemetery.
Meanwhile, UPSI vice-chancellor Prof Datuk Dr Md Amin Md Taff said on Facebook that the victim is suspected to have suffered a heart attack.
“The company will issue an official statement regarding the incident. The UPSI authorities are monitoring this matter and will provide appropriate assistance to the deceased’s family,” he said.
Earlier, Perak Fire and Rescue Department (JBPM) assistant director (Operations) Sabarodzi Nor Ahmad said that they received a call regarding the incident at 1.57am and a team was deployed to the location 10 minutes later.
According to Sabarodzi, the operation was completed at 2.36am, and the police are investigating the case. — Bernama

Title: Trailer driver charged with murder over Johor crash that killed three family members
Article:
SEGAMAT, April 7 — The trailer driver involved in the fatal crash that claimed the lives of three family members at KM212 of the Johor Bahru–Seremban Road, near the Taman Golden Hill junction in Gemas Baharu last week, was charged at the Magistrate’s Court here today.
The accused, Muhamad Shafiq Salleh, 29, nodded in understanding as the charge was read before Magistrate Amalina Johar. No plea was recorded, however, as the case falls under the jurisdiction of the High Court.
According to the charge sheet, the accused is alleged to have caused the deaths of K Myakrishnan, 72, his wife S Sevendai, 65, and Sevendai’s brother S Palamiandy, 75, at the location at 3.50 pm on April 2.
He was charged under Section 302 of the Penal Code, which carries the death penalty or imprisonment of 30 to 40 years.
If the death penalty is not imposed, the convicted person must also be sentenced to a minimum of 12 strokes of the cane.
No bail was offered to the accused, and the court set May 21 for a case mention to allow for the submission of post-mortem, medical, forensic, pathology and Puspakom reports.
Deputy public prosecutor Nur ‘Aina Muhammad Ismail Tadj appeared for the prosecution, while the accused was unrepresented.
Three people, including a married couple, were killed in the accident, while two others were injured after a trailer collided with the rear of the van carrying the victims at the Taman Golden Hill junction in Gemas Baharu.
Earlier, Transport Minister Anthony Loke directed the Road Transport Department (JPJ) and the Land Public Transport Agency (APAD) to conduct an immediate audit and investigation into the trailer operator.
Loke said strict action must be taken, including holding the company accountable for failing to monitor its driver’s conduct, noting that the driver tested positive for methamphetamine. — Bernama

Title: Anwar backs Turkiye’s call for restraint in West Asia, praises Egypt, Oman and Pakistan mediating US-Iran tensions
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 7 — Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today said Malaysia fully backs Turkiye’s advocacy for all parties to exercise restraint in the West Asia war that erupted over a month ago.
Stressing the need for engagement over escalation, the prime minister also expressed concerns that the ongoing war could potentially fuel a new arms race in an already-volatile region.
Anwar said international law should be advocated and applied consistently in order for a rules-based order to have credibility.
“A rules-based international order cannot be invoked selectively.
“It cannot protect some while denying others their legitimate rights,” Anwar said at the inaugural Kuala Lumpur-Ankara Dialogue 2026 at the Grand Hyatt Kuala Lumpur, here, today.
Anwar also commended Egypt, Oman and Pakistan for mediating between Iran and the United States to swiftly resolve the conflict that has triggered a global trade and energy crisis.
MORE TO COME

Title: Singaporean professionals fined RM1,000, ordered community service for littering cigarette butts outside Johor mall
